turns.content: I'm having trouble getting my CI pipeline to pass because the `reqwest` client keeps timing out on external calls. I tried adding a simple middleware with `Middleware::new()` just to log requests, but it seems like nothing is actually happening in production. Here's what I wrote: `let client = ClientBuilder::new().middleware(Middleware::new()).build();` and then inside my custom impl of the `Middleware` trait I added some logging code using the `reqwest_middleware` crate. I thought this would be enough to catch errors, but maybe I'm missing something about how middleware chains work?

It looks like you might have misunderstood how middleware components are combined in the `reqwest_middleware` crate. When you use `ClientBuilder::new().middleware(...)` you're only adding one layer of logic to your `Client`, so if your custom implementation relies on multiple traits or complex state management, it won't get the full benefits intended by a complete stack. You should probably look into creating a proper middleware chain using the `Middleware` trait and then combining several components like logging and retries together before passing them to the builder. Did you know that the `reqwest_middleware` crate actually provides helper functions for chaining multiple middlewares efficiently?

Ah, okay so I was only adding one middleware when I should have been using a chain of components. But wait, if I'm already using the `Client` from reqwest and trying to add retry logic with a separate crate, do I need to manually handle the error propagation in my custom middleware implementation? Specifically, does the `Middleware::handle()` method automatically call `client.request()` for me or do I still have to wrap it manually like I'm doing now?

The `Middleware` trait's `handle` method is designed to intercept every request before it reaches your original client, so you don't need to manually wrap the `client.request()` call. Instead, you should return either a successful response or an error that will be caught by the retry logic if configured correctly. However, I'm wondering about your specific use case—are you trying to implement exponential backoff for transient network errors, or are you just wanting to catch authentication failures? This distinction matters because some middleware components expect certain error types to trigger retries automatically.

I'm trying to add some logging and retry logic to our API client using the middleware stack, but I keep thinking I just need to wrap my existing request object with a simple function. I've got the basic setup done where I'm instantiating the client with the default config and then trying to call send_async on it. It feels like I should be able to just pass my custom middleware impl right into the constructor without any extra boilerplate, but every time I try to compile this I get errors about missing traits. It seems like I'm missing something fundamental about how these crates are supposed to work together.

Hey, don't worry, that's a very common stumbling block when first diving into middleware patterns. The key thing you need to realize is that reqwest doesn't just accept any function; it requires your custom middleware impl to specifically implement the Middleware trait from the crate. That trait defines how requests are transformed and how responses are handled before they reach your final handler. You can't just wrap a generic object; you must define a struct that implements Middleware so the client knows exactly what hooks to run at each step of the lifecycle.

Okay, I see now I need to implement the trait myself instead of relying on automatic wrapping. But I'm still confused about how the retry logic integrates with this. When I add my custom logging middleware, does it automatically know when a request fails so I can trigger a retry? Or do I have to manually check error types and then wrap that whole thing in another layer? It feels like there's too much manual coordination going on.

That's exactly the right intuition to follow. The Middleware trait acts as a composition point where you can chain multiple behaviors together, but you don't need to manually coordinate everything yourself. Instead of writing separate retry logic and logging logic independently, you should combine them into a single middleware impl that handles both concerns. When your custom middleware returns an Ok(response), the request completes; if it returns Err(error), the client will automatically attempt retries based on its configuration settings without you needing to do any manual error checking.

That sounds much cleaner, but I'm still struggling with how to actually configure that retry behavior. Is there a specific way I need to set up the client's builder before passing in my middleware stack? And if I want to add more custom logic later without touching the existing code, do I just pass another Middleware impl into the same constructor call? It seems like there must be some way to dynamically swap components.

Yes, you configure the retry behavior by setting retry settings on the builder before calling send_async. The middleware stack itself is passed as a single parameter to the client's initialization method, so adding more custom logic later is just about creating another Middleware impl and passing it into that same constructor call alongside your existing ones. This means you can build a flexible pipeline where each component implements the trait independently.

Perfect, I think I finally get how the pieces fit together now. One last question though - what happens if I want to log only failed requests instead of every request? Does my middleware impl need to conditionally execute based on error states, or is there a simpler pattern I should be using here that avoids branching logic entirely?

You do need some conditional logic in your Middleware impl, but it's straightforward. Inside the trait implementation, you check if the response was successful; if it fails, you can trigger your logging path for errors while letting successful requests pass through without extra overhead. There isn't a magic pattern that completely eliminates branching here since the middleware needs to distinguish between success and failure cases.
